questions.question: Explain the purpose of argc and argv in C programming. What does argv[0] always contain?
keyPoints: argc stores the total number of arguments including program name
argv is an array of character pointers storing argument strings
argv[0] always contains the program name/executable name

questions.question: Write the main function signature required to accept command line arguments and explain both parameters.
keyPoints: int main(int argc, char *argv[]) or int main(int argc, char **argv)
argc = argument count (number of arguments)
argv = argument vector (array of argument strings)

questions.question: What is the significance of argv[argc] being NULL? How does this help in processing arguments?
keyPoints: argv[argc] is always NULL (sentinel value)
It marks the end of the argument list
Allows iterating through arguments using a loop without knowing argc beforehand
Prevents buffer overflow when accessing arguments

questions.question: Write a C program that accepts two numbers as command line arguments and prints their sum.
keyPoints: Use atoi() to convert string arguments to integers
Validate argc >= 3 before processing (program + 2 numbers)
Access argv[1] and argv[2] for the two numbers
Print sum using printf()

questions.question: Why is it important to validate the number of arguments (argc) before using argv?
keyPoints: Prevents undefined behavior from accessing invalid memory
argv[1], argv[2], etc. may not exist if insufficient arguments provided
Provides meaningful error messages to users about correct usage
Program can exit gracefully with usage instructions

questions.question: Explain the difference between atoi() and strtol() functions for converting string arguments.
keyPoints: atoi() returns 0 on failure with no error indication
strtol() sets errno on conversion error and can detect failure
strtol() can handle different number bases (radix)
strtol() provides pointer to first unconverted character

questions.question: Write a complete C program that accepts a filename as command line argument, opens the file, reads its contents, and displays them on screen. Include proper argument validation and error handling.
keyPoints: Validate argc == 2 (program name + filename)
Use fopen() in read mode ("r")
Check if file opened successfully
Use fgetc() or fgets() to read contents
Close file using fclose()
Handle errors: file not found, cannot open, etc.

questions.question: Write a C program that accepts three command line arguments: two integers and an operator (+, -, *, /). Perform the specified operation and display the result. Include appropriate validation and handle division by zero.
keyPoints: Validate argc == 4
Convert argv[1] and argv[2] to integers using atoi()
argv[3] contains the operator character
Use switch-case for operations
Check for division by zero when operator is '/'
Print appropriate error messages for invalid operators

questions.question: Explain how command line arguments work in C with a diagram. Write a program that displays all arguments passed to it along with their indices.
keyPoints: Explain argc represents count including program name
argv[0] = program, argv[1] to argv[argc-1] = user arguments
argv[argc] = NULL sentinel
Loop from i=0 to i<argc to print all arguments
Show sample execution with different number of arguments

questions.question: Write a C program that accepts a number as command line argument and determines whether it is positive, negative, or zero. The program should handle invalid inputs gracefully.
keyPoints: Check argc == 2 for valid number of arguments
Use atoi() or strtol() to convert string to integer
Check if conversion was successful
Determine if number > 0 (positive), < 0 (negative), or == 0
Display appropriate message
Handle edge cases: no argument, non-numeric input
